---
title: "Resolv.conf is being overwritten."
date: 2007-06-15
forum: Networking &amp; Wireless
---

### Post by JimGvo on 2007-06-15
I installed the default Ubuntu which used dhcp.  I also installed KDE.  I then went into System/Networking and changed things to use a static IP address and a fixed DNS value.  Unfortunately I'm still getting resolv.conf overwritten.  The following message is at the top of the file:

# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN

It's a bit annoying to have to edit this file every time I reboot.  I might be able to make it read only and fix the problem, but I have a hunch the problem is a missing configuration change somewhere.   The /etc/networking/interfaces file appears to be correct.  At least it looks pretty much the same as another ubuntu system I am running that has Gnome on it, with a working static ip configuration.  Someone on irc suggested removing resolvconf but there are way too many dependcies on it by things I really don't want to lose.

Any suggestions?

Thanks,
Jm.

---

### Post by nixfaced on 2007-06-16
Hi, I got curious about this resolvconf thing and did some googling for you, and found this:

[http://www.shallowsky.com/blog/linux/resolvconf.html](http://www.shallowsky.com/blog/linux/resolvconf.html)
and
[http://linux.derkeiler.com/Mailing-Lists/Debian/2005-09/2294.html](http://linux.derkeiler.com/Mailing-Lists/Debian/2005-09/2294.html)

In a nutshell, it seems you need to add lines to your interfaces file, listing your nameservers as:

dns-nameservers <server-ip1> <server-ip2>
dns-search whatever.com

Apparently resolvconf reads this and sticks it into your resolv.conf.  

However, I still haven't figured out what resolvconf is really for.

---

### Post by Rui Pais on 2007-06-16
Hi,
try:
```
gksudo gedit /etc/dhcp3/dhclient.conf
```
and add the following line:
```
prepend domain-name-servers xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx,XXX,XXX,XXX,XXX
```
where, of course, xxx...xxx and XXX.....XXX are your primary and secondary DNSs.

---
